heading: MODULO QUINDICESIMO
card_summary: This chunk, forming part of the "Tono Ottavo" within the "Cantico Terzo," expounds Christ's imperial sovereignty under three headings: the vastness of his empire (rooted in Isaiah 9 and the Pauline letter to the Hebrews), the justice of his empire established by both hereditary right and just-war conquest, and the kingdom shared with his fellow-soldiers. Zorzi develops a sustained Kabbalistic argument that the name Yehudah (Judah) contains all four letters of the Tetragrammaton plus the letter dalet (numerical value four), signifying the divine majesty clothed in a fourfold human body of the four elements. The triple office of wisdom, priesthood, and kingship is correlated with the Hermetic figure of Hermes Trismegistus ("ter maximus") and applied to the Christian hierarchy, culminating in the papal triregnum. Extensive scriptural citation runs from Genesis 49 (Jacob's blessing of Judah, "Non sarà tolto lo scettro da Giuda") through Isaiah, the Psalms, the Gospels, Paul's epistles, Hebrews, 1 Peter 2 ("sacerdozio regale"), Hosea 4, and the Apocalypse of John, with patristic gloss from Augustine on the etymology of "episcopus."
essay:
# Chunk 130 of *De Harmonia Mundi* — Content Description and Scholarly Analysis

## Nature of the Text

This chunk is **continuous theological and philosophical prose**, not an index or bibliography. It constitutes three numbered subsections (*Concentus* I–III) within the "Modulo Quindicesimo" of the "Tono Ottavo," itself part of the "Cantico Terzo" of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*. The Bompiani 2010 edition edited by Saverio Campanini presents the Latin original on facing pages with an Italian translation by Campanini, and both versions appear interspersed in this chunk (the Latin running continuously with the Italian below each section).

## Structure and Content

**Concentus I: "Sul vasto impero del principe" (On the Vast Empire of the Prince)**

Zorzi opens by establishing Christ's universal sovereignty through a cascade of proof-texts: Paul's declaration in Hebrews that the Son was made "heir of all things," Isaiah 9:7 on the multiplication of his dominion, and Psalm 110 on enemies made his footstool. The Davidic typology is elaborated at length: just as David was anointed king by Samuel but did not immediately possess the kingdom, serving under Saul and even among foreigners, so Christ's kingdom is present but not yet fully manifest. The Kabbalistic centerpiece of this section is Zorzi's analysis of the Hebrew name יהוּדָה (*Yehudah*, Judah): it contains all four letters of the divine Tetragrammaton (יהוה) plus the inserted letter ד (*dalet*, numerical value 4), signifying that divine majesty was "hidden in a fourfold human body composed of four elements." This argument — that the tribal name of Judah encodes the Tetragrammaton — is a characteristic move of Renaissance Christian Kabbalah, linking the genealogy of the Messiah to the innermost divine Name.

**Concentus II: "Quanto è giusto l'impero di quel principe" (How Just Is the Empire of That Prince)**

Zorzi frames Christ's legitimacy through the jurisprudential distinction between *ius hereditarium* (hereditary right) and *iustum bellum* (just war). As eternal Son, Christ possesses the former; as crucified redeemer, he earns the latter through combat against sin, death, and diabolical enemies. The military metaphor is sustained through an athletic analogy (the *gymnasium* of life), the Pauline passages on the crown given only to the legitimate contestant (2 Timothy 2:5), and the long catalogue of martyrs from Hebrews 11 who "went about in sheepskins and goatskins." The Virgin Mary is treated as "more than a martyr" (*plusquam martyr*), her sword of compassion (from Simeon's prophecy in Luke 2:35) interpreted as the blade that divided her lower soul from her spirit, perfecting her. The section closes with Zacharias's canticle (Luke 1:74–75) and a quotation from Ecclesiasticus 24 on divine Wisdom holding primacy over all peoples.

**Concentus III: "Sul regno dei commilitoni" (On the Kingdom of Fellow-Soldiers)**

This section develops the concept of the *regale sacerdotium* ("royal priesthood") from 1 Peter 2:9, arguing that every Christian must simultaneously be king (ruling over earthly things and the body), priest (offering the body as living sacrifice, per Romans 12:1), and sage (possessing wisdom). The Hermetic parallel is made explicit: just as Hermes Trismegistus was "three times greatest" in wisdom, priesthood, and kingship, so Christ excels in all three, and his vicar on earth is symbolized by the *triregnum* (the papal tiara). Augustine's etymology of *episcopus* (from Greek *epi* + *scopos*, meaning superintendent or watchman over his flock) is cited. Hosea 4:6 ("Because you have rejected knowledge, I will reject you as my priest") is deployed against clerical ignorance. The section concludes with the Apocalypse 12:10 vision of Satan cast out and the gates of the kingdom thrown open to all.

## Scholarly Significance

This chunk is a prime specimen of Zorzi's synthetic method: Neoplatonic cosmology (the fourfold elemental body), Christian Kabbalah (the Tetragrammaton embedded in *Yehudah*), Hermetic tradition (Hermes Trismegistus as triple sage-priest-king), Aristotelian ethics ("every ignorant person is bad," citing Peripatetic doctrine), and exhaustive biblical exegesis are woven into a single continuous argument. The passage illustrates how Zorzi uses gematria and letter-mysticism not as ornament but as structural proof within a theological demonstration. The patristic citation of Augustine is characteristic of his method of anchoring esoteric reading in mainstream ecclesiastical authority.

## Sample Verbatim Passages

**1. The Kabbalistic argument on Yehudah (Latin):**
> "In quo nomine continentur omnes literae magni nominis quadrilitteri, aut tetragrammi interposita litera dalet, quae quatuor importat in numero. Ex quo innuitur divinitas et regia maiestas, quae continetur in Tetragrammo, habitu inventa fuisse in corpore humano quadrario ex quatuor elementis, quibus compositum est."

**2. The same passage in Italian translation:**
> "In quel nome sono contenute tutte le lettere del grande Nome quadrilittero, detto anche Tetragramma, con l'inserzione della lettera ד, dalet, il cui valore numerico è quattro. In tal modo si allude al fatto che la divinità e la maestà regia, contenute nel Tetragramma, apparve all'esterno in un corpo umano, quadruplice per via dei quattro elementi di cui è composto."

**3. The Hermes Trismegistus parallel:**
> "Cui si addamus tertium, sapientiam videlicet, erit perfectus homo, ut non triviali doctrina dicitur de Mercurio, qui erat ter maximus, in sapientia, sacerdotio, et regno. Sed multo elegantius noster summus Pontifex Christus splenduit sapientia, regno et sacerdotio."

**4. Christ's reply to Pilate as Kabbalistic gloss:**
> "Regnum autem meum non est de hoc mundo, sed supramundanam radicem habens in Archetypo, a quo descendens traxi mecum regnum, quod mysteriose innuitur in hoc nomine יהוּדָה Iehuda, ex cuius stirpe vere regia ego sum."

**5. The papal tiara as triple symbol:**
> "Unde non ab re infula, seu mitra eius triaria (quae vulgo regnum) insignitur, quasi sit triplici dyademate coronatus, sapientiae, regni, et sacerdotii."
